speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome to Challenge Coach: Notifications. I am Alex. Before you do the task, we are going to make the skill feel concrete enough to practice.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I am Jamie. I will keep asking what the learner should do, what evidence counts, and how to recover if the page does something unexpected.

speaker: ALEX
text: The skill focus is Notification hygiene, mentions, subscriptions, and avoiding overload. This is rehearsal for real contribution, so the evidence matters because it proves the move happened.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the challenge has to leave the learner with both confidence and a trail of evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Evidence is not busywork. It is how a learner, a facilitator, and a future maintainer can understand what changed and why.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, set the room for us. What are we walking into?

speaker: ALEX
text: Bonus D: Notification Mastery: For students who: Want to practice inbox management and notification configuration. The next useful detail is concrete: What you will do: Configure your GitHub notification settings for a productive workflow, then demonstrate your setup.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid project habit is to treat metadata as decision support. Labels, status, assignees, and notifications tell you what kind of attention the work needs.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next layer is this. Here is the learner-facing version. Go to github.com/settings/notifications and configure. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here are the anchors worth keeping. [ ] Email notifications: Choose which events trigger emails. [ ] Web notifications: Configure the notification inbox on GitHub.com. [ ] Watching: Review which repositories you are watching and adjust. [ ] Custom routing: If you have multiple email addresses, set up routing rules. [ ] GitHub Mobile: If you use the mobile app, configure push notification preferences.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does the learner do first, second, and then after that?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Demonstrate your setup: after configuring, answer these questions. That matters in practice: I am watching repositories because.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: How will you know when someone requests your review?; How will you avoid being overwhelmed by notifications from busy repositories?; and What is your strategy for the GitHub notification inbox (read all, triage, filter)? Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels much more doable when you say it as one move.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The magic is not speed. The magic is knowing what changed and why it matters.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now bring the learner back to the room. Anchor this part on Before: Default settings. With default settings, GitHub sends email notifications. This is the part to say slowly: Most people start ignoring all GitHub emails.

speaker: ALEX
text: For a learner, the useful signals are concrete. Every issue and PR in every repository you watch. Every comment on any thread you have participated in. Every CI status update.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What would you say to someone who is already bracing for this to be too much?

speaker: ALEX
text: Notification settings (github.com/settings/notifications) has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The parts worth keeping in working memory are these. Participating: Email ON, Web ON -- you want to know when someone replies to your conversations. Watching: Email OFF, Web ON -- browse these when you have time, not in your inbox. GitHub Actions: Email OFF for successful runs, Email ON for failed runs only.

speaker: ALEX
text: That matters because of the next idea. Repository watching has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: On the ground, that means a few things. Repositories you actively contribute to: Watch (all activity). Repositories you read occasionally: Custom (issues and PRs only). Repositories you finished with: Unwatch.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful habit is simple: orient, act, verify, then continue. That pause between action and trust is part of the work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If I am listening before the workshop starts, what should settle in my mind first?

speaker: ALEX
text: Custom routing (if you use multiple emails) has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is what that changes in practice. Route Community-Access organization notifications to your workshop email. Route personal project notifications to your personal email.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the talk moves from concept to action. Key decisions explained has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The room should hear these as checkpoints. Why email off for watching: You can check the notification bell on github.com when you choose. Email notifications for watched repos create constant interruption for low-priority updates. Why Actions failures only: A green checkmark in the PR is enough. You only need an email when something breaks. Why unwatch finished repos: Your notification feed stays relevant to current work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now it sounds like a workflow instead of a wall of instructions.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is where confidence comes from: not from never getting lost, but from knowing how to recover.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the one idea that makes the next few steps less mysterious?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. The learning objective is intentional notification management. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: If you changed at least one setting from the default and can explain why that change reduces noise while keeping you informed about what matters, you completed this bonus.

speaker: ALEX
text: Before the learner moves on. This part earns its place because see also: Appendix V: GitHub Mobile for managing notifications on your phone. That gives the learner a foothold: GitHub notifications are how GitHub tells you when something needs your attention. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How should they picture the shape of the workshop?

speaker: ALEX
text: Workshop Recommendation (Chapter 10): For this workshop, Chapter 10 is a guided practice chapter, not a graded automation chapter.

speaker: ALEX
text: If someone is taking notes, this is the short list. There are 1 guided walkthrough. Automation check: none - notification settings are account-level and cannot be validated by the Learning Room PR bot. The evidence is structured completion comment on your assigned challenge issue. The pattern is configure, filter, act.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How would you walk the room through that step by step?

speaker: ALEX
text: Chapter 10 Challenge Set has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Configure notifications and practice inbox management - set your watch level, use filters to find relevant notifications, and perform one inbox action. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Turn that into a path someone can follow.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Challenge 10.1 Step-by-Step: Notification Inbox Walkthrough: set up a useful notification workflow so you can keep up with reviews, mentions, and assignments without inbox overload. That matters in practice: the GitHub.com notifications page and your Learning Room repository settings.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the pieces that turn the idea into a usable move. Press M to mute the thread (you will not receive future updates),. Press E to mark done (removes it from inbox but you can still get future updates).

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Open your Learning Room repository on GitHub.com; Find the Watch button near the top-right of the repository page (next to Star and Fork); Activate the Watch dropdown and select Participating and @mentions. This means you only get notified when someone @mentions you or you are directly participating in a thread; and Open the notifications inbox by navigating to https://github.com/notifications (or activate the bell icon in the GitHub header). That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the ordered workflow?

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 4 checks: In the notification filters, activate the Review requested filter. This shows only notifications where someone has asked you to review their PR; Clear that filter and activate the Assigned filter. This shows notifications for issues and PRs assigned to you; Open one notification by activating its title link. Read it briefly, then navigate back to the inbox; and Perform one inbox action on a non-critical notification thread. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the kind of detail that keeps a screen reader user oriented.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The named thing - the heading, tab, field, branch, or button - is the handhold.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the moment where the page starts to make sense. Anchor this part on Completing Chapter 10: Submit Your Evidence. Open your assigned Chapter 10 challenge issue and post a completion comment. This is the part to say slowly: Close your Chapter 10 challenge issue when done. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Where do you want a learner to place their attention here?

speaker: ALEX
text: Expected Outcomes has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful version is not abstract; it sounds like this. Student can configure repository watch levels to reduce noise. Student can find review requests and assigned work quickly using filters. Student can reduce notification noise with mute or done actions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the sequence, because order matters here.

speaker: ALEX
text: If You Get Stuck has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, can't find the Watch button? It is near the top-right of the repository page, in the same row as Star and Fork. Then, notification inbox is empty? You may not have any notifications yet - that is fine. Switch to the Done tab and practice the mute/done action flow on an older notification. After that, keyboard shortcuts not working? If your screen reader intercepts M or E, click on the notification row first to give it focus, then press the shortcut. Finally, filters not showing results? Clear all filters first (click the X next to each active filter), then apply one filter at a time. Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on If You Get Stuck. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Ask facilitator to model one inbox action live, then repeat the steps yourself. Then: Finished but not sure you did it right? Compare your work against the Challenge 9 reference solution. Pause after each step and listen for the confirmation before moving on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the version that sounds like an instructor, not a manual.

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Learning Moment is still useful because notification management protects focus. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: You can stay responsive to your team without drowning in updates.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Learning Pattern Used in This Chapter. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Pattern Used in This Chapter has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Configure settings proactively (watch level) before work generates noise; Use filters to find signal in noise (review requests, assignments); Take decisive action on each notification (mute, done, or respond); and Build a daily routine that keeps your inbox manageable. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learner is not behind if they stop there and check the page.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Pausing to verify is not a detour; it is how you keep control of the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on What Generates a Notification? What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. GitHub sends you a notification when.

speaker: ALEX
text: That connects to another useful point. This part earns its place because for each repository, you choose how many notifications to receive.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Changing your watch settings for a repo. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Changing your watch settings for a repo: At the top of any repository page, find the Watch button (near Star and Fork). The next useful detail is concrete: Click it to open a dropdown with levels: Participating and @mentions, All Activity, Custom, and Ignore.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, find the Watch button in the repo header (B to navigate buttons → find "Watch [N]" or "Unwatch" button). Then, press Enter to open the dropdown. After that, press ↑/↓ to navigate the subscription options. Finally, press Enter to select your preferred level. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Changing your watch settings for a repo, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: The button label updates to confirm your choice. Then: Quick Nav B to find the Watch button in the repo header (listen for "Watch" or "Unwatch"). Next: VO+Space to open the dropdown. Last: VO+Down or arrow keys to navigate subscription options. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Tool Cards: Manage Notifications. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the learner-facing version. VS Code Desktop (GitHub Pull Requests extension). Put another way, GitHub Desktop: GitHub Desktop does not manage notifications. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Go to github.com/notifications (or press G then N). Then: Use E to mark done, I to mark read/unread, Shift+M to mute a thread. Next: The Notifications view in the GitHub sidebar shows items needing attention. Last: Click a notification to open the related issue or PR directly in VS Code. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like List PRs requesting your review (most common notification); gh search prs --review-requested @me --state open; Open the notifications page in your browser; gh browse notifications. Check your notification status (opens the GitHub notification inbox); gh api notifications --jq '.[].subject.title' head -20; View PRs that need your review (most common notification reason); gh search prs --review-requested @me --state open; View issues. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Navigating the notification list. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Navigating the notification list: the inbox shows notifications grouped by date (Today, Yesterday, This week, Older). That matters in practice: Each row shows the repository, the issue or PR title, the event type, and the time.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: D → main content landmark; H to navigate group headings (Today / Yesterday / This week / Older); Tab through individual notifications - each row announces: repo name, issue/PR title, event type, time; and Enter to open the notification (goes to the issue/PR page). The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Navigating the notification list, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 3 checks: VO+U → Main → navigate to notification list; VO+Down to move through notifications; and VO+Space to open a notification. Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a useful checkpoint before anyone starts pressing keys.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Checkpoints turn uncertainty into information.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the thread going. Anchor this part on What is announced per notification. "microsoft/vscode - Add keyboard shortcut for accessible view - @username mentioned you - 2 hours ago". This is the part to say slowly: Components: repo/org thread title event type timestamp.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Learning Cards: The Notifications Inbox. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: The Notifications Inbox has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the details that keep the idea from floating away. Press G N (two sequential keys, not simultaneous) from any GitHub page to jump directly to your notifications inbox. Press H to navigate date-group headings (Today, Yesterday, This Week, Older), then Tab through individual notification rows within each group. Each notification row announces: repository name, issue/PR title, event type (mentioned, review requested, assigned), and relative timestamp. The inbox has a three-panel layout: filters on the left, notification list in the center, and an optional detail preview on the right; at high zoom the detail pane may collapse. Unread notifications have a blue dot on the left edge of the row; read notifications do not, which is the primary visual distinction. The left sidebar filter labels (Inbox, Unread, Saved, Done) are text links that remain readable at any zoom level.

speaker: ALEX
text: Another way to ground it. Do not treat Inbox Actions - Keyboard Shortcuts as decoration. These shortcuts work when a notification is focused in the inbox. That is not trivia. These are GitHub's own keyboard shortcuts. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid navigation habit is to prove where you are before activating controls. Headings, landmarks, and the address bar are not trivia; they are your map.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Filtering the Inbox. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Filtering the Inbox is still useful because the left sidebar has quick filters.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Filtering by repository or organization. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Put Filtering by repository or organization into plain language. At the top of the notification list there is a filter/search field. The useful version is: Click the filter/search box at the top of the notification list and type a repository or organization name.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Press F or E to reach the filter input; Focus Mode → type repo name or org name; Results filter in real time; and Press Esc to clear the filter. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Filtering by repository or organization, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 3 checks: Quick Nav F to reach the filter input; VO+Shift+Down to interact → type repo or org name; and Press Esc to clear the filter and VO+Shift+Up to stop interacting. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that because it gives people permission to slow down.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is the goal. We want the page to feel explorable, not fragile.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on The "mark all as done" workflow. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. After a busy day or coming back from time away, clear your inbox methodically.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 3 checks: Open Notifications inbox; Tab to "Mark all as done" button → Enter (clears everything at once); and Then use the "Done" filter to retrieve any you want to revisit. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Muting a noisy thread. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This part earns its place because if a thread generates too many notifications. That gives the learner a foothold: screen reader users (NVDA / JAWS - Windows). That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is open the issue or PR page. Step two is in the right sidebar, scroll to the Notifications section. Step three is click Unsubscribe - you will stop receiving notifications from this thread. Step four is alternatively, from the inbox: hover over the notification row and click the mute icon (or the … menu). That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Muting a noisy thread, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: First, open the notification. Then, on the issue/PR page, navigate the sidebar to the Notifications section (H or D). After that, activate the Unsubscribe button. Finally, or from the inbox: focus the notification → press M to mute. The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Dealing with @mentions you didn't expect. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Dealing with @mentions you didn't expect: If you were @mentioned in an unfamiliar thread.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, read the thread for context before responding. Then, if it seems like a mistake, a simple "I don't think this mention was meant for me - feel free to remove it!" is enough. After that, unsubscribe after reading if you don't need to stay in the loop. The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now shift from knowing the term to using it. Learning Cards: Managing Notifications at Scale has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the lesson becomes something you can check. To mute a noisy thread from the inbox, focus the notification row with Tab and press M; you will stop receiving updates from that thread. Press E to mark a focused notification as done (archived); focus automatically moves to the next notification for rapid triage. To bulk-clear, Tab to the "Mark all as done" button at the top of the inbox and press Enter; then use the "Done" filter to retrieve anything you need later. The "Mark all as done" button is at the top of the notification list, above the first notification group; it clears the entire inbox at once. After marking notifications as done, switch to the "Done" filter in the left sidebar to review archived items; this filter persists until you switch back. On an issue or PR page, the "Unsubscribe" button is in the right sidebar under a "Notifications" heading; it prevents future notifications from that thread.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Notification Settings - Per Your Account. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Notification Settings - Per Your Account: global notification preferences are at https://github.com/settings/notifications.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the part I would want someone to say out loud while they work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. A learner should always know what they are trying to prove before they take the next action.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where confidence starts to build. Anchor this part on Starring vs. Watching - What Is the Difference? New contributors often confuse these two. This is the part to say slowly: They appear next to each other on every repository page and do completely different things. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid pull request habit is to separate three questions: what changed, why it changed, and what still needs review. That keeps the conversation useful instead of noisy.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Starring a Repository. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: starring is GitHub's equivalent of a bookmark + public endorsement. The listener should be able to check this: The star count on a repository is a community signal of popularity.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the practical turn. Do not treat Common Mistake: Accidental Watching as decoration. When you comment on an issue or PR in a repository, GitHub automatically subscribes you to that thread - but not the whole repository. That is not trivia. However, if you once click "Watch" on a busy repository (say, a popular open source project), you will receive a notification for every issue opened and every comment posted - potentially hundreds per day.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on How to silence a repository you accidentally over-subscribed to. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, How to silence a repository you accidentally over-subscribed to is still useful because this immediately reduces notifications from that repository to only threads you personally participated in.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the part worth saying out loud. Learning Cards: Starring vs. Watching has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: That becomes easier when you listen for these cues. The Star and Watch buttons are adjacent in the repository header; press B to navigate buttons and listen for "Star" or "Watch" (or "Unstar" / "Unwatch" if already active). Star: press Enter on the Star button to bookmark; this generates zero notifications and is purely a bookmark to github.com/stars. Watch: press Enter on the Watch button to open a dropdown; use Up/Down Arrow to choose a subscription level and Enter to confirm. The Star button shows a star icon and a count (e.g., "Star 1.2k"); the Watch button shows an eye icon and a count; both are in the top-right area of the repository page. After starring, the button changes to "Unstar" with a filled yellow star; after watching, it changes to "Unwatch" with a filled eye icon. At 200%+ zoom, these buttons may wrap below the repository title; they remain functional at any zoom level.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is less about memorizing and more about noticing.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Once the learner can name the move, the interface becomes much less intimidating.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on NVDA. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: NVDA has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part that makes the next action easier. The notification list is complex - use Tab to navigate individual rows rather than Browse Mode arrow keys. After marking notifications done (press E), the next notification automatically receives focus. Use NVDA+F7 → Links to get a filtered list of notification titles to scan quickly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now slow down for the part people usually miss. JAWS has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Listen for the small confirmations in this list. Like NVDA, use Tab for row navigation in the inbox. Insert+F6 (Headings list) to jump between date group headings (Today, This Week, etc.). The inbox updates in real time - JAWS will announce new notifications as they arrive.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on VoiceOver. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: VoiceOver has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: A few details make that real. Use VO+U → Landmarks → Main to reach the notification list quickly. VO+Space to activate a row, VO+Escape to return to the list. With Quick Nav on, H navigates the date group headings.

speaker: ALEX
text: Put that beside the next piece. Here is the learner-facing version. GitHub has an iOS and Android app that supports push notifications. Put another way, while the app itself is not covered as a primary tool in this workshop, it is worth knowing. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: ALEX
text: That shows up in the workshop in a few specific ways. Push notifications can alert you to review requests even when you're away from your computer. The mobile app does work with iOS VoiceOver and Android TalkBack. For primary contribution work, the desktop browser experience remains more fully featured.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Try It: Tame Your Inbox. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Try It: Tame Your Inbox: time: 2 minutes What you need: Browser, signed in to GitHub. That matters in practice: Go to github.com/notifications and practice.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Scan your inbox - Press H and Tab to navigate through notifications. Each one shows the repo name, type (issue/PR), and title; Mark one as done - Find a notification you've already read. Press E to mark it as done. It disappears from the list; and Configure watching - Go to the Learning Room repository. Press D to landmarks, find the repo nav area, then look for the "Watch" or "Unwatch" button (B to scan buttons). Choose your preferred watch level. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next point gives the learner a handle. Anchor this part on What You Accomplished Today. Day 1 is complete -- and you did a lot. This is the part to say slowly: Here is every skill you practiced, mapped to the chapter where you learned it and the evidence you created along the way.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on If This Was Your First Time. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: if today was your first time using GitHub -- or your first time using it with a screen reader -- you have already done something most developers take weeks to piece together on their own. The listener should be able to check this: You navigated a complex platform, created real contributions, and collaborated with other people in a shared codebase.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Confidence Check. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Do not treat Confidence Check as decoration. Before you close your laptop, take two minutes to answer these questions in your Chapter 10 challenge issue. That is not trivia. There are no wrong answers -- this is for you. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, which chapter felt the most natural to you? Which one do you want to revisit? Then, can you explain what a pull request does to someone who has never used GitHub? After that, if you saw a merge conflict right now, would you know where to start? Finally, what is one thing you want to try on GitHub this week that you did not get to today? That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should the learner prove to themselves after each small task?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Your Challenge Progress is still useful because look at how many challenge issues you completed today. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: Each one represents a skill you did not just read about -- you practiced it, posted evidence, and moved on.

speaker: ALEX
text: Hold that next to this. Learning Cards: What You Accomplished Today has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. You navigated GitHub entirely by keyboard: headings (H), landmarks (D), buttons (B), links (K), and form fields (F or E) became your primary navigation tools. You created issues, opened PRs, resolved conflicts, and managed notifications -- all skills that transfer to any repository on GitHub. Revisit any chapter by pressing Ctrl+L in your browser and typing the URL, or by navigating to the docs folder in the Learning Room repository. Everything you did today at your current zoom level and contrast settings will work the same way tomorrow; GitHub's layout adapts consistently to browser zoom up to 400%. If you found certain pages hard to read, revisit Settings, Accessibility on GitHub to try a different theme or motion preference before Day 2. Your profile page at github.com/your-username now shows contribution activity from today; zoom in on the contribution graph to see your green squares.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Where does the workshop stop being a tour and start becoming contribution?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. See also: Chapter 11: VS Code Interface is where Day 2 begins -- have VS Code installed and ready. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: On Day 1, you worked entirely on GitHub.com.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Between Days. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This part earns its place because if your workshop has a gap between Day 1 and Day 2, here are three optional things you can do to stay sharp. That gives the learner a foothold: GitHub Skills courses use bot-driven feedback inside pull requests. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is explore your notification settings. Now that you understand how notifications work, visit github.com/settings/notifications and customize your email and web preferences. There is no wrong configuration -- just find what feels manageable. Step two is read issues in a project you care about. Pick any open source project on GitHub and browse its issue tracker. You now know enough to understand labels, milestones, and comment threads. Notice how maintainers communicate -- you will recognize the patterns from. Step three is try a GitHub Skills course. GitHub Skills offers free, self-paced courses that run inside real repositories. "Introduction to GitHub" is a good one if you want to reinforce what you learned today. See Appendix Z for the full list of recommended courses. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on You Already Know More Than You Think. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: You Already Know More Than You Think: Think about where you started this morning. The next useful detail is concrete: You may not have known what a repository was, or how to navigate one with a keyboard, or what happens when two people edit the same file.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the final checkpoint?

speaker: ALEX
text: You should be able to point to the evidence, explain the action, and describe what you would do next if this were a real open source project. If you can teach the move back, you have learned it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if they get stuck?

speaker: ALEX
text: Read the latest message, not the loudest worry. Check the issue, the branch, the pull request, the status check, or the bot comment. Then ask for help with those facts in hand. That is how professionals collaborate.
